cards.title: Stop the LLM Slop: Builders Are Desperate for AI That Just Works (and Doesn't Break the Bank)
cards.thesis: Many people building with AI are hitting a wall because large language models (LLMs) are either too expensive or too unreliable, especially when dealing with lots of real-world data. There's a growing need for tools that make AI workflows more efficient and trustworthy, cutting down on wasted processing (tokens — the small pieces of text an AI processes) and preventing 'sloppy' or incorrect outputs.

evidence.text: Show HN: Lowfat – pluggable CLI filter that saved 91.8% of my LLM tokens. The idea is pretty simple: agents don't need the full kubectl get -o yaml or any 10k-line dump.

I'm tired of LLM skill slop, so I built mine with regression tests. How do I know if a skill or prompt is any good? How do I compare similar skills? Spotting broken software is (relatively) easy — it crashes, prints errors. But LLMs just output bad stuff.

Ask HN: Why is it still so hard for LLMs to query NoSQL databases? LLMs are good at SQL. It's precise, expressive, and unambiguous. The same cannot be said for NoSQL, and given how many people use NoSQL databases, I’m surprised there isn’t more discussion about it.

Ask HN: Do you know any company that's making money with LLMs? I am an LLM-skeptic: I think it’s an insanely good technology, propped up by the most egregious financial scam of all time. I would be open to be proven wrong though, so please: can anyone point at decent, profit making implementations of LLMs in companies?

cards.opportunity: Everyone's still figuring out how to make money with LLMs, and a huge blocker is their unpredictable outputs and high costs. The 'Lowfat' tool shows people are desperate for ways to make AI more efficient by filtering inputs, but the real goldmine is making AI *outputs* reliable. First person to ship a plug-and-play 'AI output validator' that uses a smaller, cheaper model to check if a larger model's response actually makes sense or follows a specific format will own the frustrated developer market, especially for those trying to make LLMs work reliably with messy data like logs or NoSQL outputs. You could build a simple API for this, connecting to a cheap model like GPT-3.5-turbo, and charge per validation.

cards.title: AI Dev Tools Are Blowing Up — And So Are Their Security Holes
cards.thesis: Everyone's diving headfirst into AI development, using powerful new tools like Cursor and integrating services from Claude and Gemini. But this rapid adoption is creating massive security blind spots and cost inefficiencies, from supply chain attacks hijacking popular dev setups to API key compromises that can shut down entire businesses.

evidence.text: People are actively asking for 'modern tooling' and workflows for building with AI, from total newbies to experienced developers trying to get up to speed.
A major supply-chain attack compromised an organization's GitHub, using 'Claude hooks', 'Gemini hooks', 'Cursor setup', and 'VScode tasks' to execute obfuscated malicious code.

One founder reported their $1M ARR startup was 'killed overnight' because someone got hold of an API key for Google's AI services, leading to huge unauthorized charges and Google suspending their entire account. (An API key is like a password that lets your software talk to a service like Google's AI.)

Bad design in 'MCPs' (Multi-Agent Communication Protocols – basically how different AI agents talk to each other) can cost an AI agent five times more in tokens (the units of computation AI models use, which directly translate to cost).

cards.opportunity: Everyone's rushing to adopt tools like Cursor and integrate Claude/Gemini, but that rush is creating massive security holes and burning through token budgets. Instead of just another 'AI security' tool, consider a lightweight 'AI workflow guardian' that specifically flags suspicious scripts in common AI dev setups (like .github/setup.js), helps manage and rotate API keys for AI services, and even suggests simple tweaks to agent designs to slash token costs. You'd be the first to offer peace of mind and efficiency without requiring deep security or AI ops knowledge, and you could probably build a proof-of-concept for the security scanning part this weekend.

cards.title: The AI Code Quality Crunch: Your Chance to Ship What Builders Actually Need
cards.thesis: Developers are increasingly using AI for coding, moving past initial skepticism to having 'oh shit' moments about its power. However, a constant complaint is that AI-generated code frequently introduces bugs and technical debt (issues that make code harder to maintain or extend), creating a dilemma for builders who want to ship fast but avoid future headaches.

evidence.text: There's a strong discussion about whether AI tools like Claude are actually increasing bugs in important software, with one user asking 'Did Claude increase bugs in rsync?'

Many people on Hacker News express concerns that AI 'writes bad code,' 'introduces bugs,' and 'creates technical debt,' despite a counter-argument that 'code is just a means to an end' and users don't care how it was written.

Despite the flaws, many builders have had an 'oh shit' moment, realizing that using large language models (LLMs — the AI behind tools like ChatGPT) for coding is a significant upgrade from basic code completion and replaces old resources like Stack Overflow.

cards.opportunity: Vibe coders are leaning heavily on AI to ship fast, but the constant chatter about AI-introduced bugs and technical debt is a growing problem. Instead of another AI code generator, build a 'post-AI code cleanup' tool that focuses on automatically identifying and fixing common patterns of errors or inefficiencies that AI models are known to produce, making AI-generated code truly production-ready without requiring deep debugging skills. You could start by scanning for deprecated API calls or common security pitfalls often missed by AI, giving builders confidence to ship faster.
